title: Coos County, New Hampshire

infobox.value: or **Coos County**[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([/ˈkoʊ.ɒs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), with two syllables) is the northernmost [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 31,268,[[3]](#cite_note-QF-3) making it the least-populated county in the state. The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire").[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4)

**Coös County** or **Coos County**[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([/ˈkoʊ.ɒs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), with two syllables) is the northernmost [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 31,268,[[3]](#cite_note-QF-3) making it the least-populated county in the state. The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire").[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4)

Coös County is part of the [Berlin, NH–VT Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire_micropolitan_area "Berlin, New Hampshire micropolitan area"). It is the only New Hampshire county on the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border"), south of the province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), and thus is home to New Hampshire's only international port of entry, the [Pittsburg–Chartierville Border Crossing](/wiki/Pittsburg%E2%80%93Chartierville_Border_Crossing "Pittsburg–Chartierville Border Crossing"). The only city in Coös County is [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"), with the rest of the communities being towns, or unincorporated townships, [gores](/wiki/Gore_(surveying) "Gore (surveying)") and grants.

Major industries in Coös County include [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), with the once-dominant paper-making industry in sharp decline. The county straddles two of the state's tourism regions. The southernmost portion of the county is part of the [White Mountains Region](/wiki/White_Mountains_Region "White Mountains Region") and is home to [Mount Washington](/wiki/Mount_Washington_(New_Hampshire) "Mount Washington (New Hampshire)"). The remainder of the county is known as the [Great North Woods Region](/wiki/Great_North_Woods_Region_(New_Hampshire) "Great North Woods Region (New Hampshire)"), or known locally as the North Country.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The name *Coös*, the [diaeresis](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)") indicating a disyllable, derives from a [Cowasuck](/wiki/Cowasuck "Cowasuck") word meaning "small pines".[[5]](#cite_note-5) The county government sometimes respects the diaeresis but sometimes omits it, using it on only some of its vehicles, for example. The [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire")-based weekly newspaper *The Coös County Democrat* is one of the local businesses that remains loyal to the traditional spelling.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Coös County was separated from the northern part of [Grafton County, New Hampshire](/wiki/Grafton_County,_New_Hampshire "Grafton County, New Hampshire"), and organized at [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] on December 24, 1803, although the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") was later moved to [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire"), with an additional shire town at [Colebrook](/wiki/Colebrook,_New_Hampshire "Colebrook, New Hampshire").

During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), two units of troops of the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") — [Bedel's Regiment](/wiki/Bedel%27s_Regiment "Bedel's Regiment") and [Whitcomb's Rangers](/wiki/Whitcomb%27s_Rangers "Whitcomb's Rangers") — were raised from the settlers of Coös. From the [Treaty of Paris of 1783](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") until 1835, the boundaries in the northern tip of the county (and New Hampshire itself) were disputed with [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") (which was soon to become part of the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada")), and for some years residents of the area formed the independent [Republic of Indian Stream](/wiki/Republic_of_Indian_Stream "Republic of Indian Stream").

In the 1810 census, there were 3,991 residents, and by 1870 there were nearly 15,000, at which point the entire county was valued at just under $5 million, with farm productivity per acre comparing favorably with that of contemporary [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"). Other early industries included [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and manufacturing, using 4,450 water [horsepower](/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower") in 1870.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the county has a total area of 1,830 square miles (4,700 km2), of which 1,795 square miles (4,650 km2) is land and 35 square miles (91 km2) (1.9%) is water.[[6]](#cite_note-GR1-6) It is the largest county in New Hampshire by area, and borders both [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), as well as [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

Much of its mountainous area is reserved as [national forest](/wiki/White_Mountain_National_Forest "White Mountain National Forest"), wilderness, state parks and other public areas; these encompass most of the northern portion of the [White Mountains](/wiki/White_Mountains_(New_Hampshire) "White Mountains (New Hampshire)"), including all the named [summits](/wiki/Summit_(topography) "Summit (topography)") of the [Presidential Range](/wiki/Presidential_Range "Presidential Range") (though one, [Mt. Webster](/wiki/Mount_Webster "Mount Webster"), lies about 200 feet (61 m) from the [county line](/wiki/Border "Border")). [Mt. Washington](/wiki/Mount_Washington_(New_Hampshire) "Mount Washington (New Hampshire)")'s peak is the highest in the Northeast. The 162-mile (261 km) [Cohos Trail](/wiki/Cohos_Trail "Cohos Trail") runs the length of the county.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The principal [state highways](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Highway_System "New Hampshire Highway System") in Coös County are [New Hampshire Route 16](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Route_16 "New Hampshire Route 16"), which runs mostly parallel to the Maine state line and through the city of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"), and [New Hampshire Route 26](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Route_26 "New Hampshire Route 26"), which traverses the [Great North Woods](/wiki/Great_North_Woods_Region_(New_Hampshire) "Great North Woods Region (New Hampshire)") from [Vermont Route 102](/wiki/Vermont_Route_102 "Vermont Route 102") southeast to [Maine Route 26](/wiki/Maine_Route_26 "Maine Route 26") towards [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine"). The two major [U.S. Highways](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highways "United States Numbered Highways") are [U.S. Route 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2 "U.S. Route 2"), which roughly bisects the county from [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire") to the [Oxford County](/wiki/Oxford_County,_Maine "Oxford County, Maine") line, and [U.S. Route 3](/wiki/U.S._Route_3 "U.S. Route 3"), which runs from [Carroll](/wiki/Carroll,_New_Hampshire "Carroll, New Hampshire") in the south to the [Pittsburg–Chartierville Border Crossing](/wiki/Pittsburg%E2%80%93Chartierville_Border_Crossing "Pittsburg–Chartierville Border Crossing"), where it continues as [Quebec Route 257](/wiki/Quebec_Route_257 "Quebec Route 257").

Coös County is the least populated of all New Hampshire counties, and the only one with significant amounts of [unincorporated land](/wiki/Unincorporated_land "Unincorporated land"); over half of the municipal-like entities are unincorporated townships, gores, or grants, a rarity in New Hampshire, where nearly all of the land is incorporated as [towns](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") or [cities](/wiki/City "City"). The population of these unincorporated territories is minuscule; collectively they account for less than 1% of the population of the county, with only three ([Wentworth Location](/wiki/Wentworth_Location,_New_Hampshire "Wentworth Location, New Hampshire"), [Millsfield](/wiki/Millsfield,_New_Hampshire "Millsfield, New Hampshire"), and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge,_New_Hampshire "Cambridge, New Hampshire")) reporting populations in the double digits for recent censuses. Approximately 1/3 of the population lives in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"), the only city, most populous municipality, and economic hub. [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_Hampshire "Lancaster, New Hampshire") serves as the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat").

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the county had a population of 31,268. The median age was 50.4 years. 16.3% of residents were under the age of 18 and 26.1% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 103.1 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 102.2 males age 18 and over.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-14)

The racial makeup of the county was 92.6% White, 1.7% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.3% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.0% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") and [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander"), 0.5% from some other race, and 4.3% from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"). [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") residents of any race comprised 2.1% of the population.[[14]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-14)

30.9% of residents lived in urban areas, while 69.1% lived in rural areas.[[15]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-15)

There were 13,908 households in the county, of which 21.0% had children under the age of 18 living with them and 24.7% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present. About 32.8% of all households were made up of individuals and 16.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)

There were 20,444 housing units, of which 32.0% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 71.6% were owner-occupied and 28.4% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 2.6% and the rental vacancy rate was 10.2%.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census") of 2010, there were 33,055 people, 14,171 households, and 8,879 families residing in the county.[[19]](#cite_note-census-dp1-19) The population density was 18.4 inhabitants per square mile (7.1/km2). There were 21,321 housing units at an average density of 11.9 per square mile (4.6/km2).[[20]](#cite_note-census-density-20) The racial makeup of the county was 96.9% white, 0.5% Asian, 0.4% American Indian, 0.4% black or African American, 0.3% from other races, and 1.4% from two or more races. Those of Hispanic or Latino origin made up 1.2% of the population.[[19]](#cite_note-census-dp1-19)

Of the 14,171 households, 25.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 48.5% were married couples living together, 9.2% had a female householder with no husband present, 37.3% were non-families, and 30.3% of all households were made up of individuals. The average household size was 2.23 and the average family size was 2.72. The median age was 46.4 years.[[19]](#cite_note-census-dp1-19)

18.9% of the population were under the age of 18, 6.7% were from age 18 to 24, 22.1% were from 25 to 44, 32.9% were from 45 to 64, and 19.4% were age 65 or older. The median age was 46.4 years. For every 100 females there were 103.4 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and older, there were 101.8 males.[[19]](#cite_note-census-dp1-19)

During the period 2011–2015, the largest self-reported ancestry groups in the county were 39.3% French or [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian"), 16.9% Irish, 14.2% English, 7.2% "American", 5.5% Italian, 4.9% German, and 3.6% Scottish.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

During 2011–2015, the estimated median annual income for a household in the county was $42,312, and the median income for a family was $55,385. Male full-time workers had a median income of $41,934 versus $34,859 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the county was $24,546. About 9.9% of families and 14.1% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 21.7% of those under age 18 and 8.2% of those age 65 or over.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Politics and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Politics and government")]

Coös County has supported the winner of the presidential election (at the national level) in all but three elections since [1892](/wiki/1892_United_States_presidential_election "1892 United States presidential election"). The exceptions were [1968](/wiki/1968_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_Hampshire "1968 United States presidential election in New Hampshire"), [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_Hampshire "2004 United States presidential election in New Hampshire"), and [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_Hampshire "2020 United States presidential election in New Hampshire"), when it supported [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey") over [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") over [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") over [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), respectively.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### County Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: County Commission")]

The executive power of Coös County's government is held by three county commissioners, each representing one of the three commissioner districts within the county.

| District | Commissioner | Hometown | Party |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Robert Theberge | [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire") | [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") |
| 2 | Thomas Brady | [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson,_New_Hampshire "Jefferson, New Hampshire") | [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") |
| 3 | Raymond Gorman | [Colebrook](/wiki/Colebrook,_New_Hampshire "Colebrook, New Hampshire") | [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") |

In addition to the County Commission, there are five directly elected officials: they include County Attorney, Register of Deeds, County Sheriff, Register of Probate, and County Treasurer.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

| --- | --- |
| County Attorney | John McCormick (D) |
| Register of Deeds | Leon Rideout (R) |

| Register of Probate | Terri Peterson (R) |

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[Robert Frost](/wiki/Robert_Frost "Robert Frost"), who once lived in [Franconia](/wiki/Franconia,_New_Hampshire "Franconia, New Hampshire") in neighboring [Grafton County](/wiki/Grafton_County,_New_Hampshire "Grafton County, New Hampshire"), wrote the poem "The Witch of Coös".[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Coös County is the setting for the [John Irving](/wiki/John_Irving "John Irving") novel *[Last Night in Twisted River](/wiki/Last_Night_in_Twisted_River "Last Night in Twisted River")*, Twisted River being a logging settlement in the county.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
